data.title: First Industrial Realty Trust: One Of The Best Industrial REITs
data.description:
First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) is one of my favorite real estate investment trust, or REIT, holdings, and certainly a top-tier pick in the industrial space. I've been bullish on FR since June 2024, when I first published my bullish thesis. So far, the total return amounted to over 18%, but I believe FR still has more to offer.

Let's take a look at the FY 2024 results FR published, the struggles of the industrial properties market, and the potential of the upcoming turn-around of the headwinds into tailwinds.

Quick Overview Of FR

FR leases its properties to over 880 tenants, out of which the Top 20 account for ~26.6% of rent revenue. FR concentrates on 15 key industrial markets in the US with particular interest in coastal markets.

First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. is a leading U.S.-only owner, operator, developer and acquirer of logistics properties. Through our fully integrated operating and investing platform, we provide high quality facilities and industry-leading customer service to multinational corporations and regional firms that are essential for their supply chains. Our portfolio and new investments are concentrated in 15 target MSAs with an emphasis on supply constrained, coastally-oriented markets. In total, we own and have under development approximately 69.5 million square feet of industrial space.

FR more than capitalized on the strong rent growth the industrial market experienced over the last decade. In 2023, the Company recorded an all-time-high cash rental increase of 58% in the case of lease renewals or expirations, followed by the second-highest result of 51% in 2024.

How is FR capable of raising rent so substantially? I once wondered that myself when I first got involved in the industrial market. Here's how it works - firstly, most industrial REITs keep their lease terms relatively short when compared to retail/service-oriented REITs.

Secondly, the market rent growth significantly exceeded the rent growth resulting from contractual rent escalators (typically amounting to 1% to 2% and sometimes going above that range), which allows industrial players to bump rents to much higher levels upon lease expirations or renewals. That's why they keep their lease term relatively short, which allows them to negotiate terms more often and that's why their P/FFO multiples are relatively high - they can grow rent at a much higher pace.

FR is a great example of how this dynamic works, and its results of 2023 and 2024 negotiations and lease renewals speak for themselves.

Is the industrial market a golden mine for the REITs? Not necessarily. As you can see, FR's occupancy rate dropped from nearly 99% in 2022 to 95.5-96.2% in the 2022 - 2023 period with expectations of ~95.5% occupancy in 2025. This is obviously an unfavorable tendency, which leads us to the next point: the struggles within the industrial property market.

Industrial Property Market Is Still Struggling

When we look at the data within a quarterly commercial real estate report (data for Q4 2024), we can observe a drastic fall of asking rent growth in the industrial market from ~10% in 2022 when the market was booming to a mere 2.6% on an annual basis last quarter.

How does it stand with what I wrote earlier? I think industrial REITs will still be capable of increasing rents dynamically, capitalizing on the market rent growth realized in the previous couple of years. Still, if the slowdown upholds for the years to come, we won't be able to see high double-digit rent bumps. For now, FR expects to reach 30-40% cash rent growth in 2025, according to the latest Earnings Call.

But the question remains - what's the reason behind decelerating rent growth?

As I can see in the chart below, the vacancy rate in the industrial space climbed to 6.8% after growing for 10 consecutive quarters. Look at what happened to net completions in 2022-2023 and at the same time - with net absorptions. The supply-to-demand relationship got disturbed and the market as a whole became oversupplied (it differs market-to-market).

These tendencies took a toll on occupancy rates of industrial rates, their upcoming ability to grow rent, and overall demand for industrial space.

The Signs Of Hope Keep Strengthening

There's a light in a tunnel. If you took a closer look at the chart above, you probably saw that net completions keep decreasing quarter by quarter and reached 69.2m sq. ft. (compared to over 150m in Q4 2023). The growth dynamic of the vacancy rate lost its pace. While we can still expect some growth, I expect the vacancy to peak in 2025.

On top of that, industrial space under construction dropped dynamically to 1.7% from ~4% recorded during the market's peak in 2022, hinting that the current oversupply may turn into undersupply creating a better negotiating position for landlords.

FR observes similar trends, as showcased by the chart below, highlighting that construction starts have reduced by 62% since Q3 2022.

Investment Thesis: I Remain Bullish

I put a lot of focus on FR's potential to dynamically grow its rental income and the likelihood of improving market conditions within this article, but there are a few other factors worth mentioning. Starting with a strong development pipeline secured by a vast bank of land totaling over 15m sq. ft. - that's a strong value driver for FR, which the Company will capitalize on for years to come depending on market conditions.

While there are no upcoming debt maturities in 2025, FR will face meaningful maturities in 2026 ($482m) and 2027 (over $850m). Since their average interest rates amount to 3.79% and 4.19%, respectively, FR would likely be harmed if current interest rates level uphold. Therefore, potential interest rate cuts constitute a solid catalyst for increasing FR's valuation.

Speaking about valuation, given the top-tier business metrics, likely improving market environment, manageable debt maturities, and long-term value drivers secured by land ownership, I consider FR's forward-looking P/FFO multiple of ~18.6x buyable given the potential to dynamically increase rent and dividends paid to shareholders.

Frankly, FR is a "buy" for me. Cheers!

data.title: TCW Transform Systems ETF Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Thesis

We are in the early stages of a profound shift in how the world’s energy and power are sourced, produced, transmitted, and consumed. Consumers and policy are shifting the global economy towards more efficient systems. This large-scale systems change is creating generational investment opportunities in the public markets, particularly in “brown,” old-economy businesses that are critical to driving and enabling the transformation of the systems that underpin how we use energy and power. We believe the transformation of our energy system will prove to be one of the defining investment themes of the coming decades.

Performance

In Q4 2024, PWRD† returned -1.7% net of fees, compared to 2.4% for the S&P 500. For the year ending December 31, 2024, PWRD returned 28.6%, compared to the S&P 500’s return of 25.0%. Since its inception in February 2022, PWRD has generated a cumulative return of 48.6% compared with the S&P 500’s 34.2%.

PWRD’s largest and highest conviction investments generally performed well in the fourth quarter. Investments aligned with themes such as power scarcity and grid expansion continued to deliver strong returns. The rise of AI, supply chain reshoring, and electrification of the economy is driving growth in U.S. power demand for the first time in over 20 years resulting in unique investment opportunities. A notable highlight was GE Vernova (GEV), which returned 29.1% during the quarter. Management outlined very positive prospects for growth and profitability over the medium-term and indicated that the company was largely sold out of gas turbine capacity for the next several years. Additionally, companies like Vertiv (VRT) and Vistra (VST) reported strong quarterly results, reinforcing our expectations of continued solid fundamentals. Airbus also contributed positively after overcoming supply chain challenges, with shares appreciating 9.5% in Q4 as it surpassed market delivery expectations. Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF)(OTCPK:EADSY) is an exceptionally high-quality business with massive barriers to entry operating in a global duopoly, and it continues to be one of our highest-conviction investments in the portfolio.

Canadian Pacific (CP) and First Solar (FSLR), two of our larger underperformers, were caught up in the crosshairs of the U.S. election, although each for different reasons. Shares of CP declined 15.2% after President Trump threatened tariffs as high as 25% on Mexico, which represents a large and growing market for the railroad. First Solar declined by 29.4% as the market became concerned that the new administration could look to repeal certain aspects of the Inflation Reduction Act that directly benefit FSLR.

Combined, these two investments represent less than 6% of the PWRD portfolio, and we are comfortable with the near-term headline risks given the multi-year return potential in both businesses.

Why Active Thematic ETFs?

The rise of the “Magnificent Seven” and tech stocks may lead some investors to believe they are well exposed to thematic growth – but the data tells us otherwise. Only 10% of PWRD’s portfolio holdings are captured by the S&P 500.

Investors are too tethered to indices and as a result underexposed to capturing the opportunities presented by these once-in-a-generation economic transformations. These transformations are complex and developing rapidly. As active managers we can drive the greatest returns from these megatrends by reacting quickly to new insights or when facts change.

Market Commentary

The U.S. stock market, as represented by the S&P 500 Index, delivered an impressive return of 25.0% (total return) through 2024. This positive performance was driven by corporate earnings that have exceeded market expectations and a solid macroeconomic backdrop that seemed to support the Fed’s goal to achieve a “soft landing”.

With fourth quarter earnings season kicking off, the S&P 500 is forecasted to see annual EPS growth of nearly 11% in 2024 (UBS, 01/06/2025), although the top-heavy concentration of the index certainly inflates that figure. The “Magnificent 7”, whose market capitalization consistently made up 30%+ of the S&P 500 in 2024, have buoyed EPS growth, with 2024 estimates at 36.2%, and the rest of the companies in the index (the S&P 493), with a much more modest 4.8% estimate.

Looking forward, earnings growth is expected to accelerate in 2025, while dispersion is expected to improve. In 2025, earnings for the S&P 500, Magnificent 7, and S&P 493 are projected to be 12.6%, 19.7%, and 9.2%, respectively, which we believe to be an attractive set up for us as active investors.

At a macro level, the economy appears to be on solid footing, although this had led to a sharp change in expectations for the Fed to continue its cut cycle. The unemployment rate is still historically low (4.1% in December), while average hourly earnings growth is above the historical average of the last two decades (3.9% in December). At the same time, the Fed’s progress on inflation has seemingly stalled (2.9% in December), and expectations for interest cuts in 2025 fell from ~5 cuts of ~120 bps at the start of the quarter, to ~4 cuts of ~43 bps through the end of the quarter (Bloomberg, 01/21/2025).

Finally, the Trump 2.0 administration is a major question mark for the U.S. and global economies. So far, the U.S. equity market has strongly approved of his victory while international markets have provided a more mixed response. Trump is notoriously tough on China and Mexico, and generally champions any movement that puts “America first”, however, his comments have historically often lacked follow-through. Trump and his cohorts have promised to cut regulations that are seen as “bad for [U.S.] business”, which is perhaps one of the main contributors to post-election performance.

In the present environment, we believe it remains prudent to focus on compelling secular trends which are more insulated from economic growth worries. As always, we reiterate our unswerving focus on all-weather businesses which exhibit proven management skill, pricing power, low levels of indebtedness, strong free cash flow generation, and a likely resilience in the face of a pronounced economic slowdown.

† Effective February 3, 2025, the Fund’s ticker changed from NETZ to PWRD.

data.title: OpenAI's Profit Trajectory Is An Open Question
data.description:
By Breakingviews

OpenAI’s horizons are expanding. The latest funding round of the startup behind ChatGPT, unveiled on Monday, raised up to $40 billion from investors including SoftBank Group at a $300 billion valuation – nearly double what it fetched only six months ago. Yet, the company’s path to profitability remains opaque.

Boss Sam Altman admittedly has some encouraging-sounding figures. OpenAI is hoping to generate nearly $13 billion of revenue this year, Bloomberg reported citing sources familiar with the matter, about quadruple last year. While cash flow remained negative in 2024, Altman expects this to turn positive in 2029 – when OpenAI expects to make $125 billion in revenue. Although positive operating cash flow doesn’t necessarily translate into net profit, the pathway will reassure SoftBank boss Masayoshi Son.

Yet, OpenAI looks some way off generating the revenue to cover its costs. Over 90% of its 500 million users worldwide pay nothing to use the service. In 2025, just under 5% could pay the $20-a-month charge to access the group’s more advanced AI models with fewer limitations on usage and performance, according to people familiar with the situation – generating about $5.5 billion of sales. Another 0.3% could for ChatGPT Pro, a yet-more advanced service, contributing another $3.6 billion. The trouble with this 70% chunk of OpenAI revenue is that expenses may keep rising faster than the top line.

In 2024, the computing costs of training large language models and then running them amounted to $5 billion, the Information reported in October citing an OpenAI financial document, against OpenAI revenue of only $4 billion. The cost of compute could admittedly fall given the rise of cheaper players like China’s DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK). But OpenAI’s aim to train and develop a smarter AI model means it will by the end of the decade still probably spend 60% to 80% of its annual revenue to either train or run models, the Information said.

Altman’s best workaround is to increase the $4 billion of 2025 revenue that should come from businesses. A significant chunk of this “enterprise” revenue comes from the so-called Application Programming Interface (API) arm, where the company sells direct access to their models. Since the API business charges by usage, it should be easier to cover the cost of running models. But intense pricing competition from other providers such as Mistral AI and Anthropic mean this isn’t a slam dunk, either. OpenAI’s market share of enterprise LLMs fell to 34% in 2024 from 50% a year ago, according to Menlo Ventures data.

In other words, to hit his $125 billion revenue target in 2029, Altman needs to not only grow OpenAI’s top line by 10-fold but increase his enterprise sales by even more to replace loss-making consumer business. The conveyer belt of cash from funding rounds may yet mean he can do so. But at the very least, it’s a stretch.

Context News

OpenAI said on March 31 it would raise up to $40 billion in a new funding round led by SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY) at a $300 billion valuation. The Japanese tech investment group said in a statement that it has agreed to fund OpenAI with $10 billion in mid-April and an additional $30 billion in December, contingent on the artificial intelligence firm transitioning to for-profit by the end of the year. SoftBank plans to syndicate out $10 billion of its total investment to other co-investors it did not name. A person familiar with the matter told Reuters that the remainder of the funding would come from Microsoft (MSFT), Coatue Management, Altimeter Capital and Thrive Capital. If OpenAI’s restructuring fails, SoftBank said its total investment amount in the round would drop to $20 billion. OpenAI had closed a $6.6 billion funding round in October 2024, which valued the company at $157 billion.

data.title: Could We Be Facing A New 1929?
data.description:
A complex picture

Liberation Day caught the markets off guard, and today they are in deep red following the announcement of the new tariffs. The main trading partners (China, EU, and Vietnam) have seen a significant increase in tariffs, and there will inevitably be repercussions on imports and the economy in general.

Panic is spreading on the stock market, and investors are wondering how this will play out. The main US stock indexes are heading for a fall of 20%, which is the technical threshold beyond which a recession begins. Moreover, there is no lack of analogies with the Great Depression, a period in which the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act was in force. The latter imposed tariffs of up to 60% on approximately 20,000 products and triggered an unprecedented sell-off: from peak to bottom the market collapsed by 89%.

As absurd as it may seem, in terms of tariffs, we could be in a worse situation today.

According to Evercore ISI, the effective tariff rate in 2025 is even higher than in the 1930s. How is this possible?

The reason is that the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act provided for huge tariffs, but not on all imported goods. In fact, many of them were exempt. Today, there is a 10% base for products from all countries, but in the case of China and Vietnam (the main countries responsible for the trade deficit, and therefore with a greater impact), the tariff is well over 30%.

Investors' concerns don't end there because the United States is ready to raise the stakes even higher should retaliatory tariffs be imposed:

Sit back, take it in, let's see how it goes. Because if you retaliate, there will be an escalation. If you don't retaliate, this is the high-water mark.

Scott Bessent, United States Secretary of the Treasury.

However, the world's main leaders have already announced several times that they would be willing to counter-fight as well as establish new commercial relationships with other countries. I talk about this in this article.

Overall, the situation is becoming more and more complicated due to an effective tariff rate at the level of the mid-1800s. In 200 years, the economy has changed completely, and I don't believe the economic system can sustain such high tariffs.

There are fears of a new Great Recession, but how likely is it to happen? Despite the higher tariffs, in my opinion, there is no chance of it happening, and now I'll explain why for three reasons. We probably can't even get close to it.

1) The economic context is different

Before the Smoot-Hawley Tariff was introduced, the stock market had already crashed in 1929 due to excessive speculation that had spread in previous years. To recover from that fall, the government decided to introduce high tariffs with the aim of safeguarding the US economy. It was thought that tariffs and isolationism could restart the national industry and maintain high levels of employment. On this last point, there are similarities with the current government, but the US economy is not coming out of a complicated year like in 1929. Currently, the unemployment rate is low and until a few weeks ago, the stock market was at an all-time high.

So, here we already see the first difference: the tariffs imposed by Trump are not a way to recover from an already declining economic environment. If anything, they could create it. His aim is simply to reduce the trade deficit to have more revenue, since the federal debt is too high. Either way, for now, the economy is still resilient.

The second major difference lies in the companies that comprised the US stock indexes a hundred years ago compared to today. In 1929, there were no companies like Nvidia, Meta, and Alphabet capable of generating tens of billions in free cash flow and continuous double-digit growth, as well as having more cash than debt. For these companies with such a large competitive advantage and huge profit margins, there is a limit to how far they can go, and the main US stock market indexes are heavily exposed to them.

Logically, in 1929 the technologies that we have today were lacking, and consequently the most important companies on the indexes were mainly low-margin and above all very cyclical businesses.

Energy, automobiles, and banks were the most popular sectors in those years, but none of them could resist such a heavy recession. Car sales decrease during a recession, as does the demand for energy and credit. Thousands of banks have gone bankrupt, and this has undoubtedly affected the performance of the stock market.

Even if a serious recession were to happen today, I don't believe that Nvidia would stop selling chips, or that people would stop using Facebook or watching YouTube. These are all very different economic contexts, and I don't believe a collapse of that magnitude is possible. Not even close.

2) Monetary policy and FDIC

The crisis of '29 began with the bursting of the speculative bubble, worsened with the introduction of the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, but received the final blow due to a mistake made by the Fed.

In the early 1930s, foreign investors were losing confidence in the US economy and consequently in the dollar. In an attempt to restore confidence in the dollar, the Fed decided to raise interest rates and reduce the amount of money in circulation. Needless to say, it was a disastrous decision: raising interest rates during a severe recession is the worst thing you can do.

Almost a hundred years later, the Fed has had the opportunity to experience different economic scenarios and to accumulate experience on how to deal with them; today it is impossible for it to make a similar mistake. In the event of a serious recession, rates would be lowered and most likely QE would be reintroduced to inject liquidity into the financial system. Furthermore, there is another aspect that makes 2025 incomparable to 1929.

Today we take it for granted that deposits are insured up to $250,000, and this allows us not to panic if our bank is facing hard times. But this wasn't always the case. In fact, the FDIC was introduced in June 1933 to restore confidence in the financial system during the Great Depression. Before that date, people lost their savings when a bank failed, which is why bank runs were much more common and over 9000 banks failed during that period.

Today bank runs are rare and this makes the current financial system much more stable than in 1929. When banks start to fail, then that's when we really need to worry.

3) A new investment culture

In almost one hundred years, the way of investing has totally changed thanks to the introduction of new financial products and the speed with which news circulates. Collapses like those of 1929 are not possible because companies would have to reach ridiculous multiples and I believe they would be bought out much sooner. No matter how severe the recession is, in my opinion, a company like Amazon or Alphabet cannot have a PE Ratio of 10x or 5x for a long time, it would be absurd.

Furthermore, the weight that ETFs have on the global stock market trend should not be ruled out. More and more people around the world rely on ETFs to increase their wealth over time, and this creates an underlying bullish push: accumulation plans do not stop when the market crashes.

The success of passive investing in recent years has been incredible, and I myself follow this strategy. Every month, I buy shares in the MSCI World Index, regardless of how it has performed. Moreover, the culture of investment is also spreading in Europe and is no longer just an interest of Americans.

All this was obviously not possible in 1929, not only because there were no ETFs, but also because it wasn't as easy to find relevant information. The fact that at this moment of panic you are on Seeking Alpha reading the opinions of many people already gives you an advantage over the average investor of 1929.

Conclusion

Liberation Day has scared the markets and brought the effective tariff rate to levels not seen for over 100 years. In my opinion, there is no doubt that this move will shock the world economy, but I am confident that this situation cannot last long. Scott Bessent talks about counter-tariffs in case of retaliatory tariffs from the affected countries, but frankly I don't think the US has the cards to do that. They are risking being excluded from international trade, and I believe this will hurt the American economy more than the rest of the world. By working together, the world's major leaders can cushion the damage of the new US tariffs. Isolation is never the solution as far as I'm concerned.

Assuming there are no retaliatory tariffs, the problem of inflation remains. Importing products from China and Vietnam with a 30-40% extra charge will make all the difference to the costs incurred by companies, and will probably be passed on to the end consumer. A rise in inflation would prevent the Fed from reducing rates, and the huge amount of federal debt coming due would be refinanced at high rates, condemning the government to pay enormous interest expenses. In all this, GDP estimates for Q1 2025 are plummeting lower and lower, although this figure is influenced by a number of unusual imports made before the tariffs came into effect.

In short, it's a very complicated situation, and I wouldn't rule out a recession triggered by the reduction in international trade. In any case, nothing is as disastrous as in 1929: I hope we have learned from the past and that the same mistakes will not be made. But what to do at this point?

Changing investment strategy just because there might be a recession is not a good idea because no one can know when the bottom will be reached. What I do know is that the stock market tends to grow in the long term, so I'll take advantage of this phase to increase my investments. I can't do otherwise, trying to time the market would make me lose more money than the actual market collapse.

data.title: VICI Properties: Downgrading On Relative Mispricing And Concentration Risk
data.description:
Amidst a rout in tech stocks, VICI Properties (NYSE:VICI) has held up quite strongly. The company is presumably tariff-resistant, and its lease escalators might even benefit from a surge in inflation. While there’s a lot to like about the company, including its trophy casino assets, at the end of the day, investing in REITs is typically just a numbers game. With the stock yielding just a hair over 5%, I do not view the risk-reward to be compelling here. Investors should not ignore the tenant concentration risk, and I would not be surprised to see the stock eventually re-rate downwards to properly reflect the extreme concentration. I am downgrading the stock to a neutral rating.

VICI Stock Price

I last covered VICI in July, where I upgraded the stock upon the improved valuation as well as what I viewed to be frothy conditions in the tech sector. The stock has outperformed the broader market by around 14% since then.

While it may come off as “trading,” the high predictability of this business model means that one can fairly accurately assess forward growth rates even in ultra-bullish scenarios. I see little reason to count on spectacular returns from here.

VICI Stock Key Metrics

VICI is the largest publicly traded landlord of casino assets. Caesar’s and MGM Resorts remain the company’s top tenants, making up 74% of total rent. I note that the company’s top 5 tenants made up 91% of rent.

VICI appears to have gained a loyal income investor following. This might be due in part to the company’s history of rapid dividend growth, which outpaces net lease REIT peers.

I caution that the company had previously benefited from having an under-leveraged balance sheet, which stood at just 3.1x at the end of 2021. With leverage now standing at 5.3x debt to EBITDA as of this past quarter, as well as interest rates still being elevated, I do not see any reason to expect external acquisitions to help drive accelerated growth here.

This is reflected in the numbers as well. 2024 saw AFFO per share jump 5.1% YoY to $2.26. Looking ahead, management has guided for 2025 to see up to 4% YoY growth.

While that growth rate may still outpace NNN REIT peers, it’s important to note that the company’s dividend payout ratio stands at just 64%. This means that much of its growth comes from retaining cash flow for external acquisitions. Because of this detail, it is important to value the stock on the basis of the dividend yield as opposed to an AFFO multiple to avoid double-counting.

In comparison with conventional NNN REITs, VICI’s leases have some advantages, including longer lease terms as well as faster annual lease escalators at 1.7% versus the 0.5% average.

Many of VICI’s leases have exposure to CPI, but I note that most of the lease escalators are capped at 3% or less.

Is VICI Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

Even after the recent strength, VICI still found itself trading above its past dividend yield averages. That said, I caution against hoping for a swift return to the low dividend yields of the past. As mentioned earlier, the company was able to post rapid growth due to its underleveraged balance sheet in the past.

It’s worth noting that VICI still trades richly compared to conventional NNN REIT peers. W. P. Carey (WPC) and Realty Income (O) both recently traded hands at a 5.6% dividend yield. I’d argue that both these names deserve to trade at a noted premium to VICI due to having far more diversified tenant and industry exposure. I note that VICI’s focus on experiential assets does not necessarily give it a competitive edge - or at least its recent investment activity has not suggested an ability to invest capital at higher rates of return.

Moreover, VICI stock does not look attractive in its own right. At a 5.3% yield and 4% forward growth, the stock might deliver around 9% annual returns, assuming it can maintain the current dividend yield. That kind of prospective return might be enough to merely keep up with the broader market. Given VICI’s high tenant and industry concentration, I view the prospective return as being insufficient relative to the risk. I’d get more interested in VICI if the yield expanded once again to 6% or higher. While it might sound like I am being nitpicky, I’d kindly remind readers that investing can be a numbers game. At a 6% yield, the annual return potential might rise to around 10% while additionally allowing for double-digit multiple expansion potential. At current prices, VICI does not appear to have great multiple expansion potential - if the stock does re-rate to lower yields, then I’d expect investing in other more growth-focused sectors to have offered far greater rewards.

VICI Stock Conclusion

VICI has established itself as a rising player in the NNN REIT space, but I remain unconvinced that the tenant and industry concentration risk is adequately priced into the stock. The stock is priced too richly relative to higher quality peers, and I do not see a compelling reason to count on multiple expansion from here. I am of the view that “buy and hold” investors might favor other stocks which offer greater growth and greater multiple expansion potential, traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am downgrading VICI to a neutral rating.

data.title: VICI Properties: Take A Vacation From Market Turmoil In This Safe Haven
data.description:
The S&P 500 is approaching correction territory. The Nasdaq 100 is already there. What better time to take a soothing break by investing in VICI Properties Inc. (NYSE:VICI), the primary owner of real estate along the Las Vegas strip?

No tariffs here, and no direct risk from a global trade war either, just a steady stream of growing dividends.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To VICI

VICI is an experiential REIT that owns 39 non-gaming experiential properties and 54 gaming properties, including the iconic Caesars Palace, MGM Grand, and The Venetian.

In my eyes, VICI owns the most differentiated portfolio of irreplaceable assets in the world, and it just so happens that the majority of them are situated in one of the world's most desired tourism spots - Las Vegas.

VICI has been a standout among REITs, keeping up with the market's returns since its IPO back in 2018. However, this is primarily the result of the special circumstances that led to its spinoff, which have led to a clear undervaluation in the IPO.

VICI shares peaked in August 2022, and despite a decent dividend yield, the total return for investors since has been an underwhelming 7%.

In a series of articles covering VICI, I explained that investors should understand what they're in for. I said that VICI has limited growth opportunities, and as a result, it should be viewed as a portfolio stabilizer and a safe haven rather than a driver for market-beating performance.

However, we might be in a unique situation where this might not be true.

The Setup For VICI Near-Term Suggests Outperformance

VICI has a clear mandate to invest only in differentiated experiential assets. It's what VICI's shareholders expect; it's what VICI committed to and, so far, delivered.

However, there aren't many such assets available for sale at a reasonable price, and VICI already owns a huge portfolio of these assets, valued at tens of billions of dollars.

Unless VICI completely changes its risk appetite and goes international, specifically to China, it's virtually impossible for the company to achieve earnings growth that's faster than the market. In the long term, stocks follow earnings growth.

Closing the circle leads to the conclusion that VICI shouldn't be expected to beat the market in the long term.

However, from time to time, the circumstances are such that VICI can deliver outperformance. Currently, the market is in a risk-off mode, primarily due to tariffs.

VICI has no direct exposure to tariffs. Its contracts have inflation protections, so it provides hedging against another big market concern. In addition, its tenants have shown extraordinary resilience during economic downturns. So, micro-wise, VICI is a great place to be right now.

Macro-wise, the 10-year is dropping amid recession concerns, trading at a 4.05% yield at the time of rating. This is good because lower rates make VICI more valuable and make it more attractive as a yielding asset for yield-seeking investors.

Recent Business Developments

Not too many jaw-dropping developments in VICI-land in 2024. AFFO per share rose by 5% to $2.26 in 2024, with minimal dilution compared to prior years, as VICI didn't do any major deals during the year.

The majority of VICI's business activity in 2024 was related to loans and renovations, including a $100 million loan to Homefield Kansas City, a $700 million commitment to The Venetian, and a $250 million loan to Great Wolf Resorts. In addition, in February 2025, VICI announced a deal with Cain International and Eldridge Industries to invest $300 million in a mezzanine loan.

In terms of leverage, VICI remains stable at 5.3x last-quarter-annualized net leverage ratio, based on Q4'24 adjusted EBITDA of $3.1 billion.

Looking ahead to 2025, VICI expects another relatively calm year, guiding for 4% AFFO per share growth, with little dilution, reflecting no major deals.

Valuation

At $32 a share, VICI's AFFO target for 2025 reflects a 13.7x multiple, in line with VICI's historical levels.

Meanwhile, its dividend yield is at the higher end of the historical range, and its forward EBITDA is at the lower end.

The way I see it, the current setup is probably the most favorable for investing in VICI in a long time, and I see the possibility for shares to rise to 15x forward AFFO if the 10-year yield continues to decline.

Conclusion

In the long term, VICI should be viewed as a low-risk investment that should appeal to dividend investors who are seeking to preserve capital rather than beat the market.

Currently, I believe the circumstances are such that VICI can be an outperformer in the near term while maintaining its low-risk appeal.

Therefore, I reiterate VICI as a 'Buy'.

data.title: Nvidia: The Turning Of The Tide
data.description:
The forward-looking nature of the stock market is a very simple concept, which sometimes leads to investors to a feedback loop of even more optimistic future expectations based on strong business momentum that is already reflected within the share price of a stock.

It appears that NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) would go down in history as a great example of how much a stock could deviate from its fair value, even if its sales are growing at a rate exceeding 100% and its operating margin is as high as 60%.

More importantly, it appears that a very simple indicator has marked the top of Nvidia stock with almost perfect precision. The indicator I am referring to was the inclusion of the stock in the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index in early November of last year.

On the day prior to NVDA's inclusion into the index I outlined the possibility of this event having serious repercussions for shareholder returns and the risks that shareholders were taking by buying NVDA near the $150 handle.

Not surprisingly perhaps, NVDA stock has lost roughly 30% of its value since then.

Moreover, the inclusion in the Dow Jones Index also marked the top in NVDA's share price with almost perfect precision - something that even I did not expect to occur.

As a matter of fact, this was not the first time that I wrote about the risks of investors buying a new and trendy inclusion into the index, while selling the seemingly unattractive dropout.

Back in 2020, the same thing happened with Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), which took the place of Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM). In late 2020, few people were willing to believe that Exxon would end up outperforming Salesforce, and yet that's exactly what happened after I published my thought piece.

In the case of NVDA, however, investors' expectations had driven the stock to such high levels that even the stellar business performance in recent months was not enough to support the share price.

Just shortly after I published my analysis in early November of last year, NVDA reported its Q3 2025 results and both revenue and earnings came above the already high consensus estimates.

It seemed as if my analysis was largely irrelevant in the face of the strong business momentum, and it was nearly impossible to conceive a scenario where NVDA stock would not go up as its data center revenue more than doubled from a year ago.

A few months later, the same happened when NVDA reported its Q4 FY2025 results, and once again earnings and revenue came in above the analysts' estimates.

Nonetheless, as we saw above, NVDA stock continued falling and is now hovering at around $104 a piece, down from almost $150 back just a couple of months earlier.

The Turning Of The Tide

Even though NVDA lost 30% of its value in the past few months, I would abstain from trying to call the bottom and buy the dip.

As a starting point, we haven't seen a major shift in the narrative yet, which could be foreshadowing even more downside in the coming months. On top of that, the equity market is likely to enter a bear market as I outlined last October and reiterated just a few weeks ago.

Today's headlines regarding President Trump's tariff announcement could quite easily be the catalyst that many market participants have been awaiting in order to reduce their market exposure, especially to cyclical companies and semiconductors stocks that are heavily reliant on global trade.

In light of all that, we have the stubborn sell-side analysts who are not yet rushing to downgrade NVDA. As a result, the gap between the stock price and the analysts' consensus price target has widened significantly in recent months.

Nvidia trade is still heavily once sided with "Hold" ratings almost non-existent, not to mention any "Sell" ones.

But just as I am writing these lines, the news of HSBC downgrading NVDA has emerged, and it is not unlikely that more sell-side analysts would follow in the face of having embarrassingly high target prices on the stock.

As so often happens, a potential wave of downgrades would most likely be accompanied by a well-written narrative and the impact of tariffs might just be the much-needed scapegoat.

Investor Takeaway

The recent performance of NVDA is a clear example of why investors should not succumb to emotions and remain critical of heavily one-sided trades during times when the equity market is trading at record high valuations. Additionally, investors should also pay more attention to decisions regarding the addition or exclusion of certain stocks into popular equity indices as this often marks tops for the former and bottoms for the latter group. With NVDA now trading 30% below its early November 2024 highs, I do not expect the stock to return to its $150 level anytime soon, and more downward pressure is not something that I would rule out.

data.title: Western Asset US Municipal Bond Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The investment-grade municipal market posted negative returns in the fourth quarter as yields move higher in sympathy with taxable fixed income asset classes following the presidential election, as markets looked forward to the potential for pro-growth policies that could lead to longer-term rates remaining higher for longer. Meanwhile, stronger-than-expected US economic data demonstrated a resilient economy and saw concern grow that the disinflation trend across major developed market (DM) economies may have stalled. While the Federal Reserve (Fed) cut policy rates by 25 basis points (bps) on two separate occasions in November and December, policymakers suggested two more policy rate cuts are expected in 2025, below what the market had been anticipating. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned -1.22%, leading year-to-date (YTD) total returns lower to 1.05%. Longer-duration municipals generally underperformed amid the rate volatility. Quality returns within the investment-grade segment were mixed, but high-yield municipals outperformed, returning -1.08% for the quarter and closing 2024 at a strong 6.32%.

From a technical perspective, municipal new-issue supply maintained a record pace in the fourth quarter, increasing 25% year-over-year (YoY) to $121 billion. Municipal demand weakened over the quarter, as municipal mutual fund and ETF flows recorded $15.6 billion of net inflows, according to ICI, decreasing 8% from Q3, partially driven by seasonal tax selling pressures. Looking at 2025, most market participants are expecting another elevated supply year, driven by ongoing refunding activity. Western Asset anticipates supply will reach approximately $450 billion, which could be well absorbed by expected maturities, coupon payments and the potential for an uptick in demand as investors turn to the municipal market for attractive after-tax income opportunities.

Municipal fundamentals have remained resilient, supported by elevated tax collections. In the fourth quarter, the Census released 3Q24 state and local tax collection estimates. Through the third quarter of 2024, 12-month trailing tax collections increased 6.4% YoY to $2.1 trillion, marking a record-high level, according to the Census data. On a 12-month trailing basis, individual income tax collections increased 6.6% YoY, corporate income tax collections increased 6.7% YoY, and sales tax collections increased 1.5% YoY. 12-month rolling property tax collections, the primary source of revenue for local municipalities, also recorded a strong trend higher, growing 7.7% YoY.

Performance Scorecard

We thought that … Therefore, we … And the results …
Yields would edge lower over time as growth slowed and inflation would continue to decline. We still believed that duration could serve as a valuable diversification strategy and risk-off hedge Trimmed but maintained a long-duration position versus the benchmark Duration positioning had a negative impact on performance as yields rose. -
Revenue-backed municipal sectors would offer better risk-adjusted value versus general obligation counterparts. Maintained an overweight to revenue-backed sectors, including transportation, lease-backed, and industrial-backed sectors. Security selection within revenue-backed sectors generally contributed to performance. +
Favorable credit conditions would continue to support investment-grade municipal fundamentals. Maintained an overweight to lower investment-grade and high-yield securities An overweight to lower-investment-grade and high-yield contributed to returns +

Investment Outlook

Elevated interest rate volatility and record municipal supply in 2024 have contributed to higher tax-exempt income opportunities and improved relative value compared to other fixed income asset classes in 2025. Compared to taxable Investment-grade fixed income, the after-tax relative yield pick-up remains above the 5-year averages. As the Fed is expected to continue its rate-cutting cycle into 2025, we anticipate the municipal investor base will expand as investors seek these higher longer duration after-tax income opportunities if front-end, cash-equivalent rates move lower. While we expect favorable credit conditions to remain supportive of lower-investment-grade and select high-yield allocations, considering tighter credit spreads, we anticipate opportunities to be more limited and greater scrutiny will be required.

Important Information

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Please note that an investor cannot invest directly in an index. Unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges.

Fixed income securities involve interest rate, credit, inflation and reinvestment risks, and possible loss of principal. As interest rates rise, the value of fixed income securities falls. High-yield bonds are subject to greater price volatility, illiquidity and possibility of default. Certain investors may be subject to the Federal Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT), and state and local taxes may apply. Capital gains, if any, are fully taxable.

U.S. Treasuries (USTs) are direct debt obligations issued and backed by the "full faith and credit" of the U.S. government. The U.S. government guarantees the principal and interest payments on U.S. Treasuries when the securities are held to maturity. Unlike U.S. Treasury securities, debt securities issued by the federal agencies and instrumentalities and related investments may or may not be backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. government. Even when the U.S. government guarantees principal and interest payments on securities, this guarantee does not apply to losses resulting from declines in the market value of these securities.

Credit quality is a measure of a bond issuer's ability to repay interest and principal in a timely manner. The credit ratings are provided by Standard and Poor's, Moody's Investors Service and/or Fitch Ratings, Ltd. and typically range from AAA (highest) to D (lowest), or an equivalent and/or similar rating. Investment-grade bonds are those rated Aaa, Aa, A and Baa by Moody's Investors Service and AAA, AA, A and BBB by Standard & Poor's Ratings Service, or that have an equivalent rating by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization or are determined be of equivalent quality.

Tax-related statements, if any, may have been written in connection with the promotion or marketing of the transaction(s) or matter(s) addressed by these materials, to the extent allowed by applicable law. Any such taxpayer should seek advice based on the taxpayer's particular circumstances from an independent tax advisor.

The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index is a flagship measure of the USD-denominated tax-exempt bond market over 1 year to maturity. The index includes four main sectors: state and local general obligation bonds, revenue bonds, insured bonds, and prerefunded bonds. Securities must be rated investment grade (Baa3/BBB-/BBB- or higher).

The Bloomberg Municipal Taxable Bonds Index is a flagship measure of the USD-denominated taxable municipal bond market over 1 year to maturity. Securities must be rated investment grade (Baa3/BBB-/BBB- or higher).

data.title: S&P 500: Tariffs Shock Could Send Traders Dashing For The Exit
data.description:
One of the biggest fears that the financial markets have been grappling with over the past few weeks has finally materialized. Steeper-than-expected tariffs announced by the Trump administration on imports from over 180 countries have dashed all hopes that Trump was only bluffing about initiating a full-fledged trade war with the world.

At the time of writing, S&P 500 Index (SPX) futures suggest that the market could potentially gap lower by as much as -3.2% at the open.

We think the financial market is right to be deeply concerned with the latest developments. In fact, we are surprised that the SPX is not already in correction territory, given the rapidly deteriorating U.S. economic outlook.

Red Flags Of A Recession

Since March 3, we have published several articles warning of an imminent equity market correction and an increasingly inevitable recession.

Initially, we saw early warning signs that the U.S. consumer was becoming more pessimistic about economic prospects in the near term. Deteriorating consumer sentiment readings also led to the Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta's GDPNow tracker warning that U.S. GDP may have contracted in Q1 2025.

Then came the Trump administration's back-and-forth on tariffs, which perhaps gave market observers some hope that Trump may walk back on some of his tariff threats. Nonetheless, significant damage had already been done to the economy. Uncertainties surrounding Trump's trade policies were not only negatively impacting business sentiment in the short term, but also holding back capital expenditure and hiring plans as businesses adapt to a more volatile trade environment.

Lacking positive catalysts that could potentially reverse the rapidly deteriorating economic environment, we eventually downgraded our rating on the SPX to "Sell" on March 6 and reiterated our "Sell" rating on March 13. We called for an imminent 10-15% correction, and added that we would not rule out a bear market, given how stretched valuations on the SPX have become.

Although we are happy to see an increasing number of Wall Street analysts adopting our view of a recession since, we still think the stock market is too complacent.

This Time Is Different, It Is Worse

From our perspective, the current economic environment is more precarious than in late 2022, when an overwhelming majority of Wall Street analysts predicted a recession. Of course, we avoided the recession in 2022 and 2023 thanks to several factors, including the lingering effects of aggressive fiscal stimulus, a tight labor market, and, most importantly, resilient consumer spending. But we think this time is different.

Not only is U.S. monetary policy still moderately restrictive for economic growth – which is akin to keeping a foot on the brakes even though there are signs that the engine is stalling – but the potentially inflationary impact of tariffs would prevent the Federal Reserve (Fed) from cutting interest rates, even when they are desperately needed.

Investors should also note that, from a monetary policy perspective, the economic environment in late 2022 was much more benign for Fed policymaking. By October 2022, not only had inflation been on a steady decline for several months, which gave confidence that inflation would be tamed eventually, but consumer spending was healthy. The economy was generally in good shape despite higher interest rates.

In contrast, Trump's latest round of tariffs is widely expected to increase prices for businesses and consumers. Although some Fed officials have explained that tariffs are more likely to cause a one-off price increase rather than create sustained inflation, this still means that it will take longer for the Fed to achieve its inflation target of 2%.

With the Fed unable to ease monetary policy by cutting rates and benefits from deregulation and tax cuts unlikely to come in time to save the economy, we simply see no way out of a recession.

Increasing Risks Of A Disorderly Sell-Off

If one believes that a recession will be bad news for the SPX's outlook in the near term, the Trump administration's latest shot at the global trade order would be the nail in the coffin. Now that Trump has initiated a trade war with the world, retaliatory tariffs will be coming to the U.S. In the aftermath of it all, businesses and consumers will be the ones to bear the pain.

Meanwhile, we see increasing risks of a sharp sell-off triggering margin calls and a disorderly unwinding of leveraged positions in the next few trading sessions, which could send traders dashing for the exit. With pre-market futures indicating a potentially sharp sell-off in the magnitude of -3% at the open, there is just no telling how ugly the market could get by the end of today.

data.title: Bumbershoot Holdings 2024 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners,

It was shaping up to be a solid year—until December… when we got hit in the face by a two-by-four.

I can now spend the rest of this letter trying to explain away that outcome… that we had over 10+ companies drop more than -15% in the month. That the “market” has become incredibly narrow with a handful of mega-cap stocks & specific sub-sectors driving performance; while market breadth from the other ‘ Marginal 493’ lagged considerably. That anything outside the indices has been having a particularly tough stretch for some time. But those would just be excuses.

Or I can say how I really feel… which is that I actually believe we had a solid year—despite the circumstances of our performance. The fund remains invested into a collection of high-quality businesses that continue to perform adeptly from a fundamental perspective, with constructive and durable outlooks.

Which is the entire purpose of Bumbershoot. The goal is to make money… but the process by which to do it is to invest in great businesses with durable advantages at fair valuations. That process is far from broken…

It is important to separate between process and result. This result was not achieved by being dogmatic about profitability & valuation metrics. Quite the opposite… we were in the right places. The problem is not about identifying winners. The difference performance-wise where we ought to be from where we landed was just a handful of decisions-and-omissions.
